title: Pasta con le Sarde

overview.description: A classic Sicilian pasta dish combining sardines with wild fennel, raisins, pine nuts, saffron and toasted breadcrumbs.

ingredients: 400g sardines, cleaned and filleted, or 3 x 120g tins sardines in olive oil
1 large bunch wild fennel fronds, or 150g fennel tops
1 sachet saffron, or approximately 0.2g saffron threads
method: If using fresh sardines, clean and fillet them.
If using tinned sardines, drain them and set aside.
Soak the raisins in warm water for 10 minutes.
Drain thoroughly.
Bring a large pan of salted water to the boil.
Add the wild fennel fronds and cook for approximately 10 minutes until tender.
Remove the fennel, reserving the cooking water.
Chop the fennel finely.
Add more water to the fennel cooking water if necessary and use it to cook the pasta.
Heat the olive oil in a large frying pan over medium heat.
Add the onion and cook gently until soft and translucent.
Add the garlic and chilli flakes if using.
Cook for 1 minute.
Add the pine nuts and cook until lightly golden.
Add the raisins.
Add the chopped fennel.
Stir well to combine.
Add the saffron to the white wine and leave for a few minutes.
Pour the saffron wine into the pan.
Simmer gently until slightly reduced.
Add the sardines.
Break them into large pieces and stir gently so they partly break down while some pieces remain intact.
Season with black pepper and a little salt if needed.
Cook the pasta until just al dente.
Reserve a mugful of the pasta water, then drain.
Add the pasta to the sardine mixture.
Toss gently, adding a little pasta water if necessary to loosen the sauce.
For the toasted breadcrumbs:
Heat the olive oil in a small frying pan.
Add the breadcrumbs.
Toast until golden and crisp.
Serve the pasta topped with the toasted breadcrumbs.

learning.did-you-know: Pasta con le Sarde is associated particularly with Palermo and is one of Sicily's classic dishes. Its combination of sardines, wild fennel, raisins, pine nuts and saffron reflects the island's complex Mediterranean and Arab culinary influences.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How sweet, savoury, aromatic and briny flavours can be combined in a pasta sauce, and how toasted breadcrumbs can provide a traditional alternative to grated cheese.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A distinctive Sicilian seafood pasta that introduces the children to sardines in a flavourful but approachable format, while making use of ingredients already present in the pantry.
chapman-tips: The fennel cooking water is worth using to cook the pasta because it adds another layer of flavour.
Tinned sardines work very well for a convenient family version.
Don't mash all the sardines completely; some larger pieces give the dish better texture.
The raisins should add small bursts of sweetness rather than make the dish taste sweet.
Toast the breadcrumbs carefully because they can burn quickly.
Traditional versions often use wild fennel, but fennel fronds are a practical substitute.
The toasted breadcrumbs are important—they provide texture and are sometimes called 'poor man's Parmesan'.
Don't add Parmesan or other cheese; the breadcrumbs are the traditional finishing element.
notes.batch-cooking: Better fresh. The pasta loses its texture and the sardine sauce becomes heavier when reheated.
notes.leftovers: Best eaten fresh, but leftovers can be gently reheated with a splash of water. They can also be turned into a frittata.
